source.title: introduce cutover-marker and sequence IDs to stabilize SSE handoff and JSON completion
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description:
Motivation: Intermittent production incidents showed that when a model switch or function/tool-call injection occurs mid-stream, downstream SSE consumers occasionally saw truncated JSON objects or duplicate sentinel events. Root causes included: (1) partial-object chunks being flushed out-of-order by proxies during cutover, (2) ambiguous empty-event delimiters emitted by the compatibility shim, and (3) lack of an idempotent reassembly key for retries and reconnects.

This change introduces two coordinated primitives to make handoff deterministic and safe: a cutover-marker event and a sequenced fragment ID (sequence_id + fragment_index) attached to every SSE data event emitted by the compat layer. The cutover-marker is a dedicated SSE event emitted immediately before a model/variant switch or when the runtime needs to inject a tool/function-call sentinel mid-stream. Consumers can use the marker as a safe boundary: reassembly buffers must consider any fragments with sequence_id lower than the cutover as final and drop fragments that start after the cutover with missing earlier fragment_index values.

Implementation summary:
- sse/encoder: add fields sequence_id (monotonic per-stream) and fragment_index (per sequence) on the event meta header. This is opt-in for downstream parsers via the compat header X-Redwood-SSE-Seq.
- cutover-marker: a small, explicit SSE event type "cutover" is emitted synchronously before any model switch or tool-call injection. The payload is a tiny JSON blob {"seq":"<sequence_id>","reason":"variant-swap|tool-insert"}.
- parser reassembly: consumer-facing parser now uses sequence_id+fragment_index to reassemble event payloads deterministically and tolerantly; out-of-order or overlapping fragments are reconciled against the latest cutover marker. We limit reassembly buffer to 256KB per stream to avoid OOM during adversarial clients.
- delimiter normalization: shim now normalizes contiguous newline delimiters and treats 0-length events as no-op rather than emit empty JSON tokens. This avoids spurious empty payloads when intermediaries coalesce chunk boundaries.
- compat shim: older clients that do not opt into X-Redwood-SSE-Seq will receive cutover-marker events but no sequence headers; parser heuristics fall back to brace-completion heuristics for JSON fragments to preserve compatibility.
- tests: new deterministic integration harness simulates three failure modes: interleaved chunk delivery, delayed cutover marker, and proxy-rewrite that coalesces multiple chunk boundaries. Added fuzz tests that run 6k randomized fragment permutations to validate reconstruction invariants.

Backward compatibility and rollout: The changes are additive; non-stream clients and downstreams that do not parse the new fields are unaffected. We feature-flag sequence headers behind runtime config redwood.compat.sse.seqid.enabled (default true in staging, ramped to true in prod after 48h bake). Cutover-marker emission is enabled by default with a short TTL semantics and documented in the compatibility guide.

Testing and verification: Unit tests cover the encoder/decoder roundtrip, the buffer-limits, and the fallback brace heuristics. Integration tests run against the full compat stack and the webhook consumer emulator. We exercised the change during a canary rollout: three internal services (chat, codegen, and rerank) observed zero JSON truncation events versus ~0.12% observed pre-fix under synthetic adversarial chunking.

Operational notes: If downstream consumers implement strict ordering assumptions, they should parse sequence_id and respect cutover-marker boundaries. The compatibility page (docs/compat/sse-cutover.md) documents expected semantics and examples for popular client libraries. This PR also includes a migration note and a simple parser snippet for the Node & Python SDKs.
commits: 9a4f21d - sse: add sequence_id + fragment_index to encoder headers and wire tests
7cda3b4 - compat: introduce cutover-marker event and normalize delimiters
3e8a2f9 - parser: deterministic reassembly using seqid, limit buffer to 256KB
b12f8c6 - tests: add interleaving/fuzz harness and deterministic fixtures
d65b4a1 - docs: add cutover semantics and example parsers for SDKs
review_comments: Ethan Park: requested changes - please add a unit test that validates the parser drops overlapping older fragments when a newer cutover arrives. Also prefer a smaller default reassembly buffer (256KB seems okay but add a constant to tunables).
Maya Chen: responded - added buffer constant and new unit test (parser: deterministic reassembly) in commit 3e8a2f9. Left a note in operational docs about tunables.
Lina Rodriguez: question - does cutover-marker order still hold if the proxy delays events? What guarantees do we make?

Maya Chen: reply - cutover-marker is emitted synchronously and should arrive before subsequent fragments from the new sequence, but proxies that reorder are covered by sequence_id reconciliation; documented fallback behavior in docs/compat/sse-cutover.md.
Arjun Patel: approved - tests look robust, CI green, and rollout plan is sensible. Recommend squash merge after final smoke in staging.
source.release_notes: Fix SSE streaming edgecases where partial JSON or duplicate/empty delimiters could cause truncated or mis-assembled events. Adds a cutover-marker event and sequence_id+fragment_index headers to make reassembly deterministic. Backwards compatible with fallback heuristics.

source.title: Align error shapes with SDK taxonomy (request_id, code, retryable)
source.author: Ethan Park
source.release_notes: OpenAI-compat endpoints now return Redwood-standard error fields (request_id, code, type, retryable) consistently across routes; Retry-After is propagated on 429 where available. This is additive and intended to improve cross-language SDK classification and logging.
source.description:
Context: As part of the SDK parity initiative, we need consistent error shapes across all endpoint paths so Python/TS/Go can reliably classify retryability, surface request_id, and respect Retry-After. Today the OpenAI-compat layer returns a mix of OpenAI-style error bodies and legacy gateway bodies depending on route (/v1/chat/completions vs /v1/embeddings vs /v1/responses compatibility shims), which causes SDK mismatches (e.g., TS retry middleware classifies based on error.code; Python expects request_id always present; Go relies on retryable flag + status). This PR aligns OpenAI-compat error formatting to the shared SDK error taxonomy while preserving OpenAI-compatible top-level structure for clients that parse `error.message`.

What this changes (high level): 1) Normalizes error response envelope for all OpenAI-compat routes to include `error` + Redwood fields: `request_id` and `error.code` consistently. 2) Adds `error.retryable` derived from status code + platform error code (so SDKs can make deterministic decisions without duplicating classification heuristics). 3) Propagates `Retry-After` header (seconds or HTTP date) to the client unchanged; also mirrors a parsed value into `error.retry_after_ms` when present (non-authoritative; header remains source of truth). 4) Ensures `request_id` is present for non-streaming and streaming paths (including cases where upstream closes mid-stream and we only have partial context). 5) Unifies mapping from upstream Redwood error codes to OpenAI error `type` and `code` fields, documented in-code and covered by fixture tests.

Non-goals: This does not change gateway behavior or introduce new error codes. It also does not implement client-side retry logic; the intent is to provide stable signals consumed by SDKs and conformance fixtures.

Implementation notes: - Introduced a single `toSdkTaxonomyError()` helper that takes (status, upstream body, headers) and returns a normalized object. - Preserves OpenAI-style `error: { message, type, param, code }` while adding Redwood fields at the top level: `request_id`, and adding `retryable` + `retry_after_ms` inside `error` (additive). - For legacy upstream errors that already include `request_id` in a different location, we normalize and de-dupe. - For errors produced inside openai-compat (validation, unsupported params), we generate a synthetic Redwood-like code (e.g., `invalid_request`) but keep `retryable=false`.

Testing: - Added fixture-based tests used by the cross-language conformance suite: golden JSON bodies for 400/401/403/404/409/422/429/500/502/503 and for a simulated network upstream timeout mapped to 502. - Added streaming error regression tests ensuring that when a stream fails before first token we return a standard JSON error body; when it fails after emitting tokens we terminate the SSE stream with an `event: error` payload that includes `request_id` and `error.code` (SDKs treat this as non-retryable by default; docs cover idempotency). - Verified that existing OpenAI client libraries still parse `error.message`.

Follow-ups: - Once merged, SDK conformance fixtures can stop special-casing openai-compat paths and rely on the same golden error schema everywhere. - Platform PRs in `redwood` repo handle consistent `Retry-After` emission and request_id propagation; this PR assumes those headers/fields may or may not exist and normalizes accordingly.

Screenshots/log samples: - Example 429 response now includes headers `Retry-After: 2` and JSON: `{ "request_id": "req_01J...", "error": { "message": "Rate limit exceeded", "type": "rate_limit", "code": "rate_limited", "retryable": true, "retry_after_ms": 2000 } }`.

Risk: Low. Changes are additive and mostly unify fields; clients depending on legacy shapes should continue to work since `error.message` remains unchanged. Security note: no secrets are added; we explicitly redact upstream debug fields and avoid echoing auth headers.
commits: openai-compat: add sdk-taxonomy error normalizer and mapping table
openai-compat: propagate request_id and Retry-After consistently
tests: add golden fixtures for status-code matrix + retry_after formats
streaming: emit SSE error event with request_id/code on mid-stream failure
docs(internal): note openai-compat error parity expectations for SDK conformance
conversation: Allison Grant (review): Can we ensure `retryable` is derived the same way as the SDK standard (429 + 5xx + certain network errors) and not just `status>=500`? Also please confirm `Retry-After` parsing supports both seconds + HTTP-date, and that we never treat missing/invalid Retry-After as 0ms (that can create tight loops).

Ethan Park (author): Yep. Updated `isRetryable()` to match the current SDK standard: 429, 500/502/503/504, and a small set of upstream transport categories (timeout, connection reset) mapped to 502/504 equivalents. For Retry-After: we keep the header as source of truth; `retry_after_ms` is only present when parse succeeds; otherwise omitted (not 0). Added fixtures for both formats and invalid values.

Marcus Lin (review): Please confirm we are not leaking upstream error `details` that may include internal hostnames or tokens. Also ensure we never include the raw Authorization header in logs when formatting errors.

Ethan Park (author): Good call. `toSdkTaxonomyError()` now whitelists fields from upstream body; we drop `details`, `debug`, and any non-string nested fields by default. No auth header logging here; the error handler only logs `request_id`, `status`, `error.code`, and a redacted message. Added an explicit test asserting debug fields are not present in the response.

Ibrahim Khan (review): This looks aligned with the SDK error taxonomy. One nit: `error.code` should be stable and match the enum used in conformance tests; can we map legacy `rate_limit_exceeded` to `rate_limited`? Also: for streaming mid-flight, are we sure emitting an SSE `event: error` is acceptable for our clients?

Ethan Park (author): Mapped legacy codes to the canonical enum (rate_limit_exceeded -> rate_limited; auth_error -> unauthorized; etc.) and documented the mapping table. On streaming: we already emit an error-like terminal chunk in some paths; this change makes it deterministic and includes request_id/code. Dana confirmed TS client can surface it; Go/Python treat it as a terminal error and do not auto-retry streaming by default.
Kaitlyn Nguyen (review): For 401/403, please ensure `retryable=false` always and that we don't include hints about which env var was missing (security).

Ethan Park (author): Updated: auth errors are always non-retryable. Message remains generic ("Unauthorized") and does not mention env vars / header names. SDKs can still show their own local auth-resolution guidance.
Allison Grant (review): Approving after the retryable logic update + fixtures. Please add one more fixture for 503 with Retry-After to validate we preserve the header.
Ethan Park (author): Added `test/fixtures/errors/503-retry-after-seconds.json` and assertion for header passthrough.
Allison Grant (review): Approved.
Marcus Lin (review): Approved.
Ibrahim Khan (review): Approved.

source.title: fix-sse-framing-boundary-loss-and-partial-json-emit
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Fixes several edge cases where SSE event boundaries could be lost and partial JSON payloads (function_call/structured outputs) corrupted when network chunks split mid-event. Improves parser robustness and adds telemetry to detect regressions.
source.description:
Motivation: In production we observed a class of intermittent failures where streaming OpenAI-compatible responses (function calls / structured JSON) decoded incorrectly when the underlying SSE stream split in the middle of a JSON token or delimiter. Symptoms: user-facing truncated function_call arguments, invalid JSON parse errors, and flakiness in downstream rerankers. Root cause: the SSE reassembly logic sometimes coalesced heartbeat/chunk delimiters and trimmed what looked like redundant newlines, which in edge cases removed the only event terminator for a partial 'data:' payload. That combined with naive prefix-buffering produced partial JSON emissions to the downstream decoder.

Summary of changes: 1) Introduce an explicit per-connection frame buffer state machine (frame_buffer_state) that guarantees we never drop the final event terminator; instead we canonicalize delimiters (CRLF or LF) into a single internal marker and emit an explicit frame boundary token to downstream consumers when an event is complete. 2) Add sequence-tag verification (frame_seq_id) to detect and drop/re-request malformed reassembly windows (non-destructive fallback to JSON-repair mode). 3) Preserve raw chunk edges for at-most-once reassembly debug mode (disabled by default) to aid future hotfixes. 4) Harden function_call sentinel extraction: we now wait until a balanced JSON object is observed or until a small byte-budget timeout before emitting a partial function_call to the tool layer; if timeout triggers we emit a safe failure object rather than malformed JSON. 5) Add extensive unit + fuzz tests that simulate arbitrary chunk splits, CR/LF coalescing, and interleaved heartbeats. 6) Add telemetry: sse.frame_repair_triggered, sse.partial_json_errors, and sse.frame_seq_mismatch.

Technical notes (high level): - Old behavior: trim runs of "\n" between events then call downstream immediately; this could remove the only separator when a chunk boundary occurred between the last token and the separator. - New behavior: normalise all newline sequences to "\n" internally but treat event terminator as "\n\n" canonical boundary. Incoming raw chunks are appended to frame_buffer; we scan for the canonical boundary and only then mark event complete. If a chunk boundary prevents us from seeing the completion, we keep the buffer until the next chunk and never emit partial JSON without either a balanced-JSON check or the repair path. - Balanced JSON check: cheap bracket counting plus string-aware scanning; avoids full parse on hot path. - Repair mode: attempts to stitch common split tokens ("function_call":, trailing commas, escaped quotes) and increments sse.frame_repair_triggered. If repair fails after 3 attempts / 50ms, emit structured error object to the consumer rather than raw invalid bytes.

Small illustrative snippet (pseudo): "if (hasCanonicalBoundary(buffer)) { emit(eventFrom(buffer)) } else if (balancedJson(buffer)) { emit(eventFrom(buffer)) } else { scheduleRepairOrTimeout() }"

Files changed (high level): - sse/parser_v2.go: new frame state machine, boundary normalization, repair logic. - sse/streamer.go: upstream chunk append behaviour, telemetry hooks. - openai/compat_layer.go: caller-facing changes: delayed emit until safe, new error envelope for partial-function failures. - sse/fuzz_tests.go: dozens of new fuzz cases for chunk splits and heartbeat interleaving. - sse/integration_test.go: e2e tests with openai-compat harness. - docs/compatibility.md: documented new failure envelope and rollout notes. - telemetry/metrics.toml: new metric definitions.

Testing and validation: - Unit: 120 new cases covering split boundaries, CRLF vs LF vs mixed chunks. - Fuzz: fed corpus derived from production logs (redacted) reproduced original failure deterministically. - Integration: staged canary under production workload emulator for 24h; partial_json_errors fell from avg 16/day to 0-1/day in canary cohort. - Load/perf: microbenchmarked parser hotpath; added ~2.6% CPU overhead under worst-case pathological streams; we kept the previous SLOs by only invoking the heavier balanced-JSON check when necessary.

Rollout plan: - Feature flag sse.frame_v2 enabled server-side for 10% of traffic for 48h, monitor sse.partial_json_errors and latency 95/99. - After verification, gradated ramp to 100% over 72h. - If sse.frame_seq_mismatch spikes, automatically rollback the flag and open a hotfix.

Related: ENG-3675 (investigate streaming function-call regressions), ENG-3720 (add telemetry for SSE reassembly).

Checklist:
- [x] unit tests added
- [x] fuzz tests added
- [x] e2e integration validated in staging
- [x] metrics and alerts added
- [x] documented fallbacks in compatibility guide

Commit summary: see commits field for details.

Notes: I intentionally opted for an explicit boundary marker and a small repair-mode rather than optimistic trimming — this trades a tiny CPU cost for correctness for structured outputs which are high-value for customers. title: fix-sse-framing-boundary-loss-and-partial-json-emit
preview:
Motivation: In production we observed a class of intermittent failures where streaming OpenAI-compatible responses (function calls / structured JSON) decoded incorrectly when the underlying SSE stream split in the middle of a JSON token or delimiter. Symptoms: user-facing truncated function_call arguments, invalid JSON parse errors, and flakiness in downstream rerankers. Root cause: the SSE reassembly logic sometimes coalesced heartbeat/chunk delimiters and trimmed what looked like redundant newlines, which in edge cases removed the only event terminator for a partial 'data:' payload. That combined with naive prefix-buffering produced partial JSON emissions to the downstream decoder.

Summary of changes: 1) Introduce an explicit per-connection frame buffer state machine (frame_buffer_state) that guarantees we never drop the final event terminator; instead we canonicalize delimiters (CRLF or LF) into a single internal marker and emit an explicit frame boundary token to downstream consumers when an event is complete. 2) Add sequence-tag verification (frame_seq_id) to detect and drop/re-request malformed reassembly windows (non-destructive fallback to JSON-repair mode). 3) Preserve raw chunk edges for at-most-once reassembly debug mode (disabled by default) to aid future hotfixes. 4) Harden function_call sentinel extraction: we now wait until a balanced JSON object is observed or until a small byte-budget timeout before emitting a partial function_call to the tool layer; if timeout triggers we emit a safe failure object rather than malformed JSON. 5) Add extensive unit + fuzz tests that simulate arbitrary chunk splits, CR/LF coalescing, and interleaved heartbeats. 6) Add telemetry: sse.frame_repair_triggered, sse.partial_json_errors, and sse.frame_seq_mismatch.

Technical notes (high level): - Old behavior: trim runs of "
" between events then call downstream immediately; this could remove the only separator when a chunk boundary occurred between the last token and the separator. - New behavior: normalise all newline sequences to "
" internally but treat event terminator as "

" canonical boundary. Incoming raw chunks are appended to frame_buffer; we scan for the canonical boundary and only then mark event complete. If a chunk boundary prevents us from seeing the completion, we keep the buffer until the next chunk and never emit partial JSON without either a balanced-JSON check or the repair path. - Balanced JSON check: cheap bracket counting plus string-aware scanning; avoids full parse on hot path. - Repair mode: attempts to stitch common split tokens ("function_call":, trailing commas, escaped quotes) and increments sse.frame_repair_triggered. If repair fails after 3 attempts / 50ms, emit structured error object to the consumer rather than raw invalid bytes.

Small illustrative snippet (pseudo): "if (hasCanonicalBoundary(buffer)) { emit(eventFrom(buffer)) } else if (balancedJson(buffer)) { emit(eventFrom(buffer)) } else { scheduleRepairOrTimeout() }"

Files changed (high level): - sse/parser_v2.go: new frame state machine, boundary normalization, repair logic. - sse/streamer.go: upstream chunk append behaviour, telemetry hooks. - openai/compat_layer.go: caller-facing changes: delayed emit until safe, new error envelope for partial-function failures. - sse/fuzz_tests.go: dozens of new fuzz cases for chunk splits and heartbeat interleaving. - sse/integration_test.go: e2e tests with openai-compat harness. - docs/compatibility.md: documented new failure envelope and rollout notes. - telemetry/metrics.toml: new metric definitions.

Testing and validation: - Unit: 120 new cases covering split boundaries, CRLF vs LF vs mixed chunks. - Fuzz: fed corpus derived from production logs (redacted) reproduced original failure deterministically. - Integration: staged canary under production workload emulator for 24h; partial_json_errors fell from avg 16/day to 0-1/day in canary cohort. - Load/perf: microbenchmarked parser hotpath; added ~2.6% CPU overhead under worst-case pathological streams; we kept the previous SLOs by only invoking the heavier balanced-JSON check when necessary.

Rollout plan: - Feature flag sse.frame_v2 enabled server-side for 10% of traffic for 48h, monitor sse.partial_json_errors and latency 95/99. - After verification, gradated ramp to 100% over 72h. - If sse.frame_seq_mismatch spikes, automatically rollback the flag and open a hotfix.

Related: ENG-3675 (investigate streaming function-call regressions), ENG-3720 (add telemetry for SSE reassembly).

- [x] unit tests added
- [x] fuzz tests added
- [x] e2e integration validated in staging
- [x] metrics and alerts added
- [x] documented fallbacks in compatibility guide

The change is backwards-compatible for consumers that only expect textual streaming; we add a new structured error envelope for partial-function failures so clients can handle gracefully.

source.title: Align token counting + usage semantics with OpenAI-compatible endpoints
source.author: Nadia Rahman
source.release_notes: OpenAI-compatible endpoints now return usage (prompt_tokens/completion_tokens/total_tokens) computed from the same serialized prompt + sampled completion that feed the billing meter. Streaming usage is emitted once (final chunk) and no longer double-counts tool-call arguments or repeated deltas.
source.body:
## Context
We’ve had recurring customer confusion + disputes where:
- Invoice/usage export values don’t match what they see in OpenAI-compatible responses under `usage`.
- Streaming requests (especially with tool/function calling) report inconsistent or inflated `completion_tokens`.
- Retries/fallbacks can cause the compat layer to report usage for an attempt that wasn’t the billed attempt.

This PR aligns OpenAI-compatible `usage` reporting with Redwood’s internal token metering semantics for the Hosted API, using the same tokenization inputs we use for billing.

Relevant threads/tickets: SUP-28451 (invoice tokens do not match usage API), SUP-28463 (double-charged retries), ENG-4430 (double-counting on retry path), PM-1842 (meter sanity KPIs).

## What changes
### 1) Single source of truth for usage computation
- Introduces a dedicated `UsageCalculator` for OpenAI-compatible endpoints that takes:
  - the exact serialized prompt string used for the downstream model call (after messages->prompt formatting), and
  - the exact completion text/tool payload returned to the client.
- `usage.prompt_tokens`, `usage.completion_tokens`, `usage.total_tokens` are computed from the model’s tokenizer (not OpenAI’s), but now match the numbers we record for billing for the same request.

### 2) Fix double counting in streaming + tool calls
Previously, the compat streaming handler would:
- count tokens per delta and then also count the final assembled completion, and/or
- count tool-call arguments once when streamed and again when the final tool call object was emitted.

New behavior:
- We only compute usage from the final assembled response object.
- For streaming responses, we emit `usage` exactly once in the final chunk (OpenAI-style), and we do not update any running token totals in intermediate deltas.

### 3) Retry/fallback alignment
- If the downstream request is retried or routed to a fallback model, the compat layer now attaches the usage to the billed attempt only.
- Adds propagation of the internal `metering_attempt_id` / `request_fingerprint` to prevent usage being computed from an abandoned attempt.

### 4) Cache attribution mapping (non-billing)
- When a request is served from Redwood prefix/KV cache and the billing pipeline reports cached prompt tokens, we now populate OpenAI-style `prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens` (when available) without changing billed totals.
- If cached token detail is not available, we do not fabricate it; totals remain correct.

### 5) Tests + golden vectors
- Adds golden test vectors for:
  - messages->prompt serialization (system/user/assistant ordering + separators)
  - tool calls (`tool_calls[].function.arguments`) including structured output JSON
  - streaming with partial chunks + client disconnect simulation
  - `n>1` (multiple choices): usage is total across choices, consistent with existing Redwood billing behavior

## Compatibility notes
- Response shape remains OpenAI-compatible. We only change how the numbers in `usage` are computed.
- We do not guarantee parity with OpenAI’s tokenizer for OpenAI models; we guarantee internal consistency between compat responses and Redwood billing meters.

## Files / areas changed (high level)
- `src/handlers/chat_completions_streaming.ts`: stop per-delta token accumulation; emit usage once at end
- `src/handlers/chat_completions.ts`: unify with shared usage calculator
- `src/usage/usage_calculator.ts`: new usage computation module + interfaces
- `src/serialization/messages_to_prompt.ts`: make prompt serialization deterministic and shared by streaming + non-streaming
- `src/middleware/retry_context.ts`: plumb billed-attempt identifiers
- `test/golden/usage_*`: golden vectors + regression tests

## Example (before/after)
Scenario: streaming tool call with arguments streamed over multiple chunks.
Before: completion_tokens inflated (~1.4x) because arguments were counted per delta and in the final assembled tool call.
After: completion_tokens == tokens in final tool call payload (counted once). Total aligns with billing export for the same request_id.

## Rollout
- Safe to roll out immediately: compat layer only.
- Observability: emits `openai_compat_usage_mismatch_total_tokens` counter comparing legacy vs new calculator (sampled at 1%) for one week after merge.
- If mismatch spikes, we can flip `OPENAI_COMPAT_USAGE_V2=false` to revert calculation while keeping the deterministic serialization helpers.

## Checklist
- [x] Unit tests for calculator + serialization
- [x] Integration tests for streaming tool calls
- [x] Added metric for legacy-vs-v2 delta
- [x] Verified against 10 invoice discrepancy samples from Finance (Acme + ZenChat cases) using request replays in staging

## Review notes / conversation
**Logan Wright:** Can you confirm we’re not changing billed totals here, just the compat response usage?
**Nadia Rahman (author):** Correct. Billing totals are unchanged; this only aligns compat `usage` with the billing meter inputs. Added the legacy-vs-v2 delta metric to watch for surprises.

**Monica Patel:** For streaming, are we always emitting `usage` in the final chunk? Some SDKs assume it’s absent.
**Nadia Rahman:** Yes, final chunk only. If a client disconnects early, we won’t be able to deliver final usage in-body; in that case we still log usage internally and attach it to the request record for exports (unchanged). Added a test to ensure we don’t emit partial `usage` mid-stream.

**Allison Grant:** The prompt serialization helper changed delimiters—does this match what we actually send to the runtime?
**Nadia Rahman:** Updated to call the same serializer used in the downstream adapter. Added golden vectors to lock formatting and a staging replay to verify parity.

**Leila Farouk:** Please add a regression case for `n=2` + tool calls; we’ve broken that before.
**Nadia Rahman:** Added `test/golden/usage_choices_tool_calls_n2.json` and wired it into CI.

## CI
Checks:
- build: PASS
- lint: PASS
- unit: PASS
- integration (streaming): PASS
- compat-contract tests: PASS

## Merge
Squash merged to `main` as 7a2c3d1.

## Commits (squash summary)
1) usage: add v2 calculator + shared serialization
2) streaming: emit usage once (final chunk), remove per-delta counting
3) retries: plumb billed-attempt context into compat handlers
4) tests: add golden vectors for tool calls, streaming, n>1
5) metrics: legacy-vs-v2 mismatch counter (sampled)

source.title: Optionally surface active optimization config version in response headers
source.author: Benji Okafor
source.release_notes: - Adds opt-in response header exposing the active Optimization Config Version used for request execution (useful for debugging canaries/rollbacks and correlating with metrics tagged by config_version).
source.description:
Context: As part of the Safe apply + rollback workstream, we need an easy way (especially for Support/SRE and advanced customers) to confirm which Optimization Config Version (batching/caching/quant profile) was active for a given request. Today, customers can infer this indirectly via dashboards or internal tooling, but it’s not visible at the request boundary.

What this PR does: Add an opt-in response header in the OpenAI-compatible proxy that surfaces the active optimization config version ID when available. This is intentionally disabled by default to avoid leaking internal identifiers to all callers.

Header contract:
- Response header name: `x-redwood-optimization-config-version`
- Value: opaque version identifier (e.g., `ocv_01JH8V3K8XW2M6H9E8RZ0G4KQY`), stable for a given immutable config version
- Only emitted when opt-in is enabled AND the underlying execution path provides a config version

Opt-in / gating:
- If request header `x-redwood-debug: true` is present AND the caller is authenticated (standard Redwood auth; no anonymous traffic), emit the response header.
- Additionally, allow service-side enablement via env var `RW_OPENAI_COMPAT_EXPOSE_OPT_CONFIG_VERSION=1` for internal environments (staging, perf-canary). When this env var is set, the response header is emitted without requiring `x-redwood-debug`.
- If config version is unknown/unavailable, header is omitted (do not emit placeholder values).

Implementation notes:
- Pull config version from upstream response metadata when present:
  - For non-streaming responses: read `x-redwood-internal-opt-config-version` from the upstream Redwood API response and map it to the public header.
  - For streaming responses: capture upstream headers before streaming begins; emit the public header at the start of the stream.
- Adds `Access-Control-Expose-Headers: x-redwood-optimization-config-version` when the header is emitted to support browser debugging tools in Console/embedded clients.
- Ensures we do not vary cache keys or request routing on `x-redwood-debug` (header is handled in the response path only).

Testing:
- Unit tests for header emission behavior (default off, request opt-in, env var override)
- Integration tests for both /v1/chat/completions streaming + non-streaming flows
- Regression test for CORS expose-headers behavior only when the public header is present

Operational impact:
- Minimal. Header emission is conditional and cheap. No changes to request routing/behavior.

Follow-ups (not in scope):
- Consider surfacing a second header for "rollout stage" (canary vs promoted) once the rollout controller semantics settle.
- Docs update in customer-facing guide (tracked in docs repo).

openai-compat: gate header behind debug request header and env var
tests: streaming/non-streaming integration coverage for header emission
cors: expose config version header when emitted
chore: add config version header to structured request logs (debug only)
source.ci_summary: Checks: lint (pass), typecheck (pass), unit tests (pass), integration tests (pass), build (pass).
conversation: Felix Schneider (review): Can we align header naming with the Console/Control Plane terminology? I worry `x-redwood-config-version` is too generic if we later add model version headers. Suggest `x-redwood-optimization-config-version` and keep value opaque.
Benji Okafor (author): Good call. Updated to `x-redwood-optimization-config-version` and removed earlier `x-redwood-config-version` alias. Value remains opaque `ocv_*`.
Marcus Lin (security review): Please ensure this is not emitted by default. Also, avoid surfacing internal header names directly. Add explicit gating and confirm it doesn’t bypass auth in any mode.

Benji Okafor (author): Added default-off gating: either `x-redwood-debug: true` on an authenticated request, or env var override for internal clusters only. Also mapped from upstream internal header `x-redwood-internal-opt-config-version` to the public header; the internal name is never forwarded.
Sean Gallagher (SRE): For streaming, we can’t set headers after the body starts. Are we guaranteed to have the upstream headers before we write the first chunk?

Benji Okafor (author): Yes—updated streaming handler to wait for upstream response headers before writing the first SSE chunk. Added integration test to validate header presence on streaming responses.
Chloe Martin (console): Please expose via CORS headers when present; otherwise browser clients can’t read it. But don’t widen expose-headers unnecessarily on all responses.
Benji Okafor (author): Implemented conditional `Access-Control-Expose-Headers` only when we emit `x-redwood-optimization-config-version`.
Felix Schneider (review): Can we add one more test for the negative case: debug header present but upstream version missing => omit header?
Benji Okafor (author): Added unit test `does_not_emit_when_upstream_missing()` and a streaming integration case where upstream omits version (simulated).
Marcus Lin (approval): Approved after gating + mapping changes. Thanks for keeping internal header private.
Sean Gallagher (approval): LGTM. This will help correlate customer reports with config_version-tagged metrics during canaries/rollbacks.
source.notes: This PR is part of the Safe apply + rollback initiative; it provides request-level traceability for config_version without requiring Console access.

source.title: Add onboarding user-agent tags for OpenAI-compat traffic attribution
source.author: Brian Murphy
source.body:
## Summary
This PR adds a lightweight, privacy-safe User-Agent tagging mechanism in the OpenAI-compat proxy so we can distinguish onboarding traffic (quickstarts/templates/Console checklist flows) from general production traffic. This improves funnel attribution for TTFS/TTFP reporting for customers using OpenAI SDKs against Redwood endpoints.

## Motivation
A meaningful portion of new Hosted API users go through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint (e.g., `openai.chat.completions.create(...)` pointed at Redwood). Today those requests are difficult to attribute to onboarding sources because the upstream UA is often generic (or absent) and we can’t reliably infer “quickstart vs prod” from payload.

We do *not* use this tag to compute checklist progress (progress remains server-side event driven per onboarding event schema/ADR); this is strictly for analytics segmentation and debugging ("is the quickstart generating 429s?", "is streaming disconnect happening on onboarding snippets?").

## Design
- The proxy continues to pass through the client-provided `User-Agent`.
- If the request includes an explicit onboarding hint header, we append a short UA token with a constrained value set.

### Onboarding hint header
- Header: `x-rw-onboarding-source`
- Allowed values (enforced in proxy): `quickstart`, `docs`, `console_checklist`, `example_app`
- If header is missing or invalid, we do not append anything.

### UA token format
- Token appended to UA: `rw-onboarding/<source>`
- Example:
  - Incoming UA: `OpenAI/JS 4.28.0`
  - With `x-rw-onboarding-source: quickstart`
  - Forwarded UA: `OpenAI/JS 4.28.0 rw-onboarding/quickstart`

### Privacy + cardinality
- No user identifiers, no tenant names, no API key fingerprints.
- Source values are enumerated and validated to avoid cardinality blow-ups in logs/metrics.

## Implementation details
- Adds a small UA builder helper that:
  1) reads/validates `x-rw-onboarding-source`
  2) appends `rw-onboarding/<source>` to the existing UA (or sets a minimal UA if missing)
  3) strips the hint header before forwarding upstream (so it doesn’t leak beyond the proxy boundary).
- Adds structured access log field `onboarding_source` (string or empty) to support querying without parsing UA.
- Adds unit tests for:
  - pass-through UA (no hint)
  - missing UA (hint present)
  - invalid hint values (ignored)
  - header stripping behavior

## Rollout / usage
- No behavior change unless clients send `x-rw-onboarding-source`.
- Follow-ups:
  - Update quickstarts/templates to send `x-rw-onboarding-source: quickstart`.
  - Console checklist deep links/snippets can set `console_checklist`.

## Verification
- Local: `make test` + `make lint`
- Staging: verified forwarded headers in proxy debug logs and confirmed UA suffix appears in request logs.

## Notes
This intentionally does not attempt to infer onboarding status from API keys or account age in the proxy. That logic lives in telemetry/eventing and cohorting, and we want the compat layer to remain dumb/portable.

Refs:
- Linear: PM-751 (TTFS/TTFP definitions), ENG-5815 (onboarding KPIs), ENG-5812 (onboarding-related SDK/quickstart updates)
- Related gateway work (separate repo): emit onboarding events at key steps
commits: compat: add onboarding source header parsing + UA token append
compat: add access log field onboarding_source
tests: cover UA tagging + header stripping edge cases
docs: add developer note for quickstarts to set x-rw-onboarding-source
review_conversation: Logan Wright: Can we guarantee this doesn’t create high-cardinality UA strings? Suggest allowlist + also log a separate field so dashboards don’t need UA parsing.
Brian Murphy (author): Added allowlist enforcement (`quickstart|docs|console_checklist|example_app`) and added `onboarding_source` field in access logs. Invalid values are ignored and not forwarded.
Nadia Rahman: Please strip the hint header before forwarding. Also confirm we’re not adding anything when hint is absent (most traffic).
Brian Murphy (author): Implemented stripping in sanitize middleware; added tests for both strip + pass-through behavior.
Marcus Lin: Security/privacy check: ensure header doesn’t accept arbitrary strings and doesn’t accidentally include request metadata. Also update docs to warn against embedding user ids.
Brian Murphy (author): Header is validated against constants; docs updated with a hard warning: do not include user identifiers; only the allowlisted values are supported.
Monica Patel: From SDK/quickstart POV, prefer a single header name across languages. This looks good; we’ll update templates to send it consistently.
Logan Wright: Approved after allowlist + log field change.

source.title: tighten-sse-segmentation-and-async-boundary-handling-for-structured-output
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.description:
Motivation: We continue to see production anomalies where streamed SSE payloads emit partial JSON tokens for structured outputs (function calls / tool outputs) when the application layer triggers async flushes or when network segmentation coalesces small writes. This PR tightens segmentation logic in the OpenAI-compat SSE layer to ensure that emitted events are idempotent and reassemblable by downstream consumers even when mid-token boundaries are split or multiple producers race to flush.

Summary of changes: Atomic event framing tokens are introduced ("::sse-evt::") to delimit logical SSE events at the transport layer; an async boundary sanitizer defers boundary emission until the current UTF-8 token and JSON structural units are complete; a small in-memory reassembly window with sequence ids protects against out-of-order fragment delivery during concurrent flushes; and function-calling sentinel handling is hardened to avoid emitting partial function argument objects. The patch keeps backward compatibility with existing consumers by continuing to emit standard SSE event fields (event/data) but adds the optional framing marker to help fragile parsers.

Why this is different: Prior fixes focused on coalescing and delimiter ordering. This change targets the race between application flushes and network write coalescing by making the server-side emitter aware of async boundaries (goroutine/task flush contexts) and ensuring JSON token integrity across chunk boundaries. It also reduces reliance on heuristics (newline counts) and uses structurally-informed flush gating for structured outputs.

Testing and validation: Unit tests simulate 1000 randomized fragmentation patterns with both short and long sequences, including injected scheduler preemptions. Integration tests run the OpenAI-compat streaming harness with a reference client that deliberately splits UTF-8 codepoints and sends partial event fragments; metrics recorded include percent of reassembled valid JSON objects and percent of function-call sentinel errors. CI includes an interop job against the node and python SDK streaming parsers to ensure no regressions to legacy clients. Observability: added a per-request counter for "sse_reassembly_attempts" and histogram for "sse_fragment_latency_ms".

Deployment plan: staged rollout — Canary on Hosted (10% traffic) for 48h with automatic rollback on >0.1% rate of structured-output parse errors; then ramp to 50% for 24h, then full. Dedicated and Private customers will receive a config flag (compat.sse.strict_frame=true) to toggle the optional framing marker while we monitor client parsing compatibility.

Checklist: [x] Unit tests added and passing locally [x] Integration harness updates [x] Canary rollout plan documented [x] Release notes drafted

Related: ENG-2123 (streaming stability), ENG-2190 (function call robustness), SUP-4871 (customer-reported partial-json incidents).

- d2b7c91: add async-boundary sanitizer and flush gating
- e8f9b2c: reassembly window with seq ids and idempotent emission
- 9f4a1d0: harden function-call sentinel emitter to avoid partial args
- 4b2d33e: add unit/integration tests for fragmentation scenarios
- 0a5c6e8: docs/compat: describe framing opt-in and rollout notes

Integration tests run the OpenAI-compat streaming harness with a reference client that deliberately splits UTF-8 codepoints and sends partial event fragments; metrics recorded include percent of reassembled valid JSON objects and percent of function-call sentinel errors. CI includes an interop job against the node and python SDK streaming parsers to ensure no regressions to legacy clients. Observability: added a per-request counter for "sse_reassembly_attempts" and histogram for "sse_fragment_latency_ms".

Deployment plan: staged rollout — Canary on Hosted (10% traffic) for 48h with automatic rollback on >0.1% rate of structured-output parse errors; then ramp to 50% for 24h, then full. Dedicated and Private customers will receive a config flag (compat.sse.strict_frame=true) to toggle the optional framing marker while we monitor client parsing compatibility.

Checklist: [x] Unit tests added and passing locally [x] Integration harness updates [x] Canary rollout plan documented [x] Release notes drafted

Related: ENG-2123 (streaming stability), ENG-2190 (function call robustness), SUP-4871 (customer-reported partial-json incidents).

source.title: client-negotiation: graceful request/response schema negotiation + streaming control header
source.author: Maya Khatri
source.release_notes: Introduce a client-driven negotiation header (Redwood-Schema) to opt into v2 behavior; add a streaming-control header (Redwood-Stream-Mode) to stabilize structured streaming and function-calling fallbacks. Backcompat shim keeps v1 parsing for servers that don't opt-in.
source.description:
Context: We need a less-disruptive path for rolling out the v2 request/response shape (structured outputs + richer function/tool calling) to OpenAI-compatible clients. Previously we relied on server-side feature flags and opt-in query params which made client routing and proxying brittle for streaming responses. This PR introduces a client-driven negotiation header plus a lightweight server backcompat shim and explicit streaming control header to make gradual adoption safer.

TL;DR: Add a Redwood-Schema header to let clients declare protocol intent (v1 | v2-beta | v2). Add Redwood-Stream-Mode to indicate how the server should stream structured tokens (raw-delta vs framed-json). Implement a server-side compatibility layer that inspects Redwood-Schema and falls back to canonical v1 envelopes when necessary.

Motivation:
- Reduce incidents where proxies or gateways rewrite streaming chunks in ways that break function-calling JSON frames.
- Give SDKs a predictable way to opt into beta behaviors behind a header rather than opaque feature flags.
- Ensure that older clients/third-party proxies continue to receive v1-compatible responses by default.

What changed (high-level):
- API surface: accept and validate two new headers: "Redwood-Schema" and "Redwood-Stream-Mode".
- Request parsing: server now normalizes incoming requests into an internal canonical model before invoking runtime.
- Response emission: streaming path picks a serializer based on the negotiated schema and stream mode.
- Backcompat shim: v1 serializer is preserved and used when header absent or explicitly v1.
- SDK changes: TS and Python SDKs updated to set Redwood-Schema when calling new helper methods (examples included in docs).
- Docs: migration section with example header usage and a troubleshooting checklist.
- Tests: added unit + integration coverage including a simulated HTTP proxy that rewrites chunk boundaries to validate resilience.

Checklist:
- [x] Header parsing and validation
- [x] v1 serializer unchanged
- [x] v2-beta serializer (structured framing) implemented
- [x] Streaming-control modes: raw-delta, framed-json
- [x] SDK examples and tests
- [x] Integration tests with chunk-rewriting proxy
- [x] Rollout gating in Console (feature flag hooks)

Commits (summary):
- core: negotiate-schema: add header parsing and canonicalization helpers
- streaming: serializer: add framed-json serializer and stream-mode switch
- openai-compat: translator: map OpenAI-style function_call to internal tool spec
- sdk-ts: expose setSchemaHeader helper and update streaming example
- sdk-py: add schema header option to client constructors
- docs: openai-compat: add migration guide and examples

Changed files (top-level view):
- server/src/http/headers.go (new header parsing/mapping)
- server/src/streaming/serializer.go (framed-json serializer)
- server/src/compat/backcompat_shim.go (shim for v1 behavior)
- server/src/compat/proxy_tests/proxy_rewrite_test.go (integration proxy test)
- sdk/typescript/src/client/schema.ts (helper to set header)
- sdk/python/redwood/client.py (constructor option)
- docs/openai-compat/migration.md (migration guide)
- docs/openai-compat/streaming.md (streaming modes)
- tests/integration/streaming_end_to_end.test.js
- ci/workflows/schema-negotiation.yml

Review notes and rationale:
- We intentionally keep v1 as the default: absence of Redwood-Schema results in v1-compatible behavior. This minimizes breakage for third-party proxies and older SDKs.
- v2-beta exposes a framed JSON stream where individual events are self-describing (type + payload) to make structured output robust over lossy intermediaries.
- Redwood-Stream-Mode supports two values: raw-delta (token deltas as before) and framed-json (self-contained JSON frames). Framed-json is the recommended mode for function-calling because it avoids partial-chunk decodes.
- We avoid embedding schema version into the URL to make CDNs and signed URLs simpler and avoid cache fragmentation.

Rollout plan (ops):
- Stage 1 (canary): enable header parsing in production and log occurrences; no change in behavior unless header present.
- Stage 2 (beta): opt-in clients set Redwood-Schema: v2-beta and Redwood-Stream-Mode: framed-json. Monitor for broken streams and parsing errors.
- Stage 3 (gradual): allow select customers to set Redwood-Schema: v2, enable minor rule relaxations in runtime (e.g., allow richer tool call bindings).
- Final: flip default to v2 only after 90 days of stability and customer migration docs.

Compatibility considerations and common pitfalls:
- Proxies that fold or coalesce chunk boundaries may still cause trouble for raw-delta streaming; framed-json solves most of these but requires clients to read by frame, not by raw bytes.
- Some HTTP libraries buffer until line endings; clients should use streaming body readers recommended in SDKs.
- WebSocket-based transports should mirror header negotiation at handshake time.

Review conversation (highlights):
- Ethan Park: "Can you add a metric that counts negotiation failures (malformed header / unsupported schema)?" — Maya: "Added redwood.net.schema.neg_fail metric and unit tests."
- Sofia Alvarez: "I'm worried about consumers accidentally opting into v2 in SDKs that default to v1. Make sure examples show explicit set." — Maya: "Examples updated, and SDKs expose explicit helpers; no default change."
- Ravi Menon: "Integration test looks brittle on CI due to timing; can we make chunk rewrites deterministic?" — Maya: "Test refactor to deterministic chunk sizes; CI flakiness addressed."

CI results and iteration:
- First CI run: lint pass, unit tests pass, integration flaky (streaming_end_to_end failed due to timing)
- After test fix: full CI pass, coverage +1.2%.

Documentation and examples included:
- docs/openai-compat/migration.md contains examples for curl, node, and python with explicit headers and fallback testing guidance.

Triage/incident notes:
- This PR was created after two incidents where function-calling responses were malformed when proxied through a third-party CDN (SUP-1123). The framed-json mode prevents those classes of failures.

Evaluation criteria for merge:
- No production behavior change without explicit headers
- Comprehensive tests for both streaming modes
- SDKs and docs updated
- Console rollout gating hooks in place
commits: add: header negotiation and canonicalizer
feat(streaming): framed-json serializer and mode switch
chore(sdk): expose schema header helper in TS and PY SDKs
test: proxy chunk-rewrite integration tests
docs: migration guide and streaming stability notes
review_comments: Ethan Park: Added metric request — implemented as redwood.net.schema.neg_fail with unit tests.
Sofia Alvarez: SDK default behavior — clarified in examples and left default as v1.
Ravi Menon: Flaky integration test — refactored to deterministic chunking sizes.
QA (Anika): Verified streaming_end_to_end with both modes behind a simulated CDN rewrite.
Security (Dmitri): Quick review, no sensitive header content; suggested logging rate limits for header abuse.
